preamble.summary: A communal lament that addresses God as the Shepherd of Israel enthroned on the cherubim. The psalm calls upon God to shine forth, to stir up His might, and to come and save. Its structure is built around a threefold refrain — 'Restore us, O God; let Your face shine, that we may be saved' — which appears in verses 4, 8, and 20, each time with an escalating divine title. The central section (verses 9-17) develops an extended vine allegory: God transplanted a vine from Egypt, cleared the ground, and let it grow until it covered the mountains and shaded the cedars. But then He broke down its walls, and every passerby plucks it. The psalm pleads for God to look down from heaven, tend the vine He planted, and restore the man at His right hand.
preamble.remarkable: The vine allegory is one of the most sustained metaphors in the Psalter. Beginning with the transplanting from Egypt (the Exodus), the vine's growth traces Israel's history through the conquest and the Davidic-Solomonic expansion, when Israel's influence stretched from the Mediterranean Sea to the Euphrates River. The vine grew to cover mountains and shade great cedars — an image of astonishing abundance. Then the reversal: God Himself broke down the walls. The devastation is not blamed on the vine's weakness or on the strength of the enemies but on God's decision to remove the protection. This makes the plea more urgent: if You broke the wall, only You can rebuild it. The threefold refrain with escalating titles — 'O God' (verse 4), 'O God of hosts' (verse 8), 'O LORD God of hosts' (verse 20) — creates a crescendo of increasingly desperate address.
preamble.friction: The psalm mentions Joseph, Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh (verses 2-3) — all northern tribes — which has led many scholars to associate this psalm with the fall of the northern kingdom to Assyria in 722 BCE. However, the vine imagery in verses 9-12 encompasses all Israel, and the plea for the 'man at Your right hand' (verse 18) may refer to the Davidic king. The psalm may therefore be a plea from the combined people, possibly written in Judah after 722 but concerned with the fate of the northern tribes. The phrase ben adam ('son of man') in verse 18 is debated: does it refer to the king, the nation personified, or a future messianic figure?
preamble.connections: The vine allegory connects to Isaiah 5:1-7 (the Song of the Vineyard), Ezekiel 15 and 17, and Jesus' parable of the wicked tenants (Mark 12:1-12) and His declaration 'I am the vine' (John 15:1-8). The shepherd imagery echoes Psalms 23, 77:21, 78:52, and Ezekiel 34. The phrase 'Shepherd of Israel' connects to Genesis 49:24, where God is called 'the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel.' The cherubim throne connects to the ark of the covenant (Exodus 25:18-22, 1 Samuel 4:4, 2 Samuel 6:2).

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֥חַ אֶל־שֹֽׁשַׁנִּ֗ים עֵ֫ד֥וּת לְאָסָ֥ף מִזְמֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Shoshannim-Eduth, A Psalm of Asaph.
verses.rendering: For the director of music. To 'Lilies of Testimony.' A psalm of Asaph.
translator_notes: The phrase el shoshannim edut ('to the lilies of testimony') likely indicates a tune or musical setting. Shoshannim ('lilies') appears in the superscriptions of Psalms 45, 60, 69, and 80, possibly referring to a melody by that name. The addition of edut ('testimony, witness') distinguishes this setting from the others.

verses.text_hebrew: רֹעֵ֣ה יִשְׂרָאֵ֣ל הַאֲזִ֑ינָה נֹהֵ֖ג כַּצֹּ֥אן יוֹסֵ֗ף יֹשֵׁ֥ב הַכְּרוּבִ֗ים הוֹפִֽיעָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Give ear, O Shepherd of Israel, thou that leadest Joseph like a flock; thou that dwellest between the cherubims, shine forth.
verses.rendering:
O Shepherd of Israel, hear us!
You who lead Joseph like a flock,
You who are enthroned on the cherubim — shine forth!
verses.expanded_rendering: Three titles open the psalm. Ro'eh Yisrael ('Shepherd of Israel') identifies God by His primary relationship to the people: He tends them, feeds them, guides them, protects them. Noheg katson Yosef ('who leads Joseph like a flock') specifies the northern tribes — Joseph represents Ephraim and Manasseh. Yoshev hakkeruvim ('enthroned on the cherubim') locates God on the mercy seat of the ark, between the golden cherubim whose wings form God's throne (Exodus 25:22). The imperative hofi'ah ('shine forth!') is a theophanic command: appear in glory, manifest Your presence, let Your radiance break through.
translator_notes: The phrase yoshev hakkeruvim ('enthroned on the cherubim') appears in 1 Samuel 4:4, 2 Samuel 6:2, and Isaiah 37:16. It is the most precise liturgical description of God's throne location — between the wings of the two cherubim that crowned the ark of the covenant.

key_terms.semantic_range: shepherd, one who feeds, tends, pastures, guides, and protects a flock
key_terms.note: The title occurs only here in the Psalms, though the concept permeates the Psalter (Psalms 23, 28:9, 74:1, 78:52, 79:13, 95:7, 100:3). It is the most intimate title for God in this psalm — the divine king is first a caretaker of vulnerable creatures.

verses.text_hebrew: לִפְנֵ֤י אֶפְרַ֨יִם ׀ וּבִנְיָמִ֣ן וּמְנַשֶּׁ֗ה עוֹרְרָ֥ה אֶת־גְּבוּרָתֶ֑ךָ וּ֝לְכָ֗ה לִישֻׁעָ֥תָה לָּֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Before Ephraim and Benjamin and Manasseh stir up thy strength, and come and save us.
verses.rendering:
Before Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh,
stir up Your might
and come to save us!
verses.expanded_rendering: The three tribes named — Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh — are the three tribes that marched directly behind the ark during the wilderness journeys (Numbers 2:18-24). The psalmist is invoking the march formation: when the ark moved forward, these tribes followed. The imperative orerah et gevuratekha ('rouse Your might!') is a battle cry — wake up Your power, mobilize Your strength. The verb ulkhah ('and come!') is an urgent plea for God to march with His people as He did in the wilderness.
translator_notes: The specific mention of Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh (the Rachel tribes) rather than Judah has led many scholars to read this as a northern psalm, possibly composed after the Assyrian destruction of the northern kingdom in 722 BCE. Benjamin, located between north and south, could represent the border territory caught between the two kingdoms.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֥ים הֲשִׁיבֵ֑נוּ וְהָאֵ֥ר פָּ֝נֶ֗יךָ וְנִוָּשֵֽׁעָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Turn us again, O God, and cause thy face to shine; and we shall be saved.
verses.rendering:
Restore us, O God;
let Your face shine, that we may be saved.
verses.expanded_rendering: The first occurrence of the refrain. Three elements: hashivenu ('restore us, turn us back, bring us back') — the plea for return, both physical and spiritual; ha'er panekha ('make Your face shine') — the language of the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:25), meaning divine favor, attention, and warmth; venivvashe'ah ('and we will be saved, and let us be saved'). The logic is precise: if God turns toward the people with favor, salvation follows automatically. The problem is not the people's weakness but God's averted face.
translator_notes: The refrain appears three times with escalating divine names: Elohim (verse 4), Elohim Tseva'ot (verse 8), YHWH Elohim Tseva'ot (verse 20). Each repetition adds weight to the address, as if the psalmist is trying progressively more powerful names to get God's attention.

key_terms.semantic_range: to illuminate, to make bright, to cause to shine; the face represents presence, attention, favor
key_terms.note: The 'shining face' of God is the opposite of the 'hidden face' (hester panim). When God's face shines, He is present, attentive, and gracious. When His face is hidden, His people experience abandonment and catastrophe. The phrase comes from the Aaronic blessing in Numbers 6:25.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהִ֣ים צְבָא֑וֹת עַד־מָתַ֥י עָ֝שַׁ֗נְתָּ בִּתְפִלַּ֥ת עַמֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD God of hosts, how long wilt thou be angry against the prayer of thy people?
verses.rendering:
O LORD God of hosts,
how long will You smolder against Your people's prayer?
verses.expanded_rendering: The full title YHWH Elohim Tseva'ot ('LORD God of hosts/armies') is deployed immediately — the most comprehensive divine name, combining the covenant name (YHWH), the title of deity (Elohim), and the military title (Tseva'ot, 'of hosts/armies'). The question ad matai ('how long?') is the standard lament interrogative. But the specific accusation is startling: ashanta bitfillat ammekha ('You smoke/smolder at the prayer of Your people'). God is angry not at the people's sin but at their prayer. The smoke of divine anger rises against the very act of approaching Him. This is the deepest possible crisis: even prayer provokes God.
translator_notes: The verb ashan ('to smoke') is the same used in Psalm 74:1 for God's anger smoking against His flock. The image is of a God whose wrath is so intense that the prayers offered by His people are received with hostility rather than grace.

verses.text_hebrew: הֶ֭אֱכַלְתָּם לֶ֣חֶם דִּמְעָ֑ה וַ֝תַּשְׁקֵ֗מוֹ בִּדְמָע֥וֹת שָׁלִֽישׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou feedest them with the bread of tears; and givest them tears to drink in great measure.
verses.rendering:
You have fed them the bread of tears
and made them drink tears by the measure.
verses.expanded_rendering: The Shepherd who should feed His flock has instead given them lechem dim'ah ('bread of tears') — their food is weeping, their sustenance is grief. The parallel: vattashqemo bidma'ot shalish ('You have made them drink tears by the shalish'). The word shalish is a unit of liquid measure (perhaps a third of a larger measure) — tears are measured out in precise quantities, as if grief were being rationed. The One who gave manna now gives sorrow.
translator_notes: The image of eating and drinking tears inverts the provision narrative of Psalm 78:23-25, where God opened heaven's doors and rained down bread. The same God who fed the people with grain from heaven now feeds them with the bread of affliction.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּשִׂימֵ֣נוּ מָ֭דוֹן לִשְׁכֵנֵ֑ינוּ וְ֝אֹיְבֵ֗ינוּ יִלְעֲגוּ־לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou makest us a strife unto our neighbours: and our enemies laugh among themselves.
verses.rendering:
You have made us an object of strife to our neighbors,
and our enemies mock us.
translator_notes: The word madon ('strife, contention, quarrel') means the neighbors fight over Israel — not in defense but in derision. The mockery (yil'agu, from la'ag, 'to mock, to scoff') echoes Psalm 79:4. Israel has become entertainment for the surrounding nations.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֣ים צְבָא֣וֹת הֲשִׁיבֵ֑נוּ וְהָאֵ֥ר פָּ֝נֶ֗יךָ וְנִוָּשֵֽׁעָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Turn us again, O God of hosts, and cause thy face to shine; and we shall be saved.
verses.rendering:
Restore us, O God of hosts;
let Your face shine, that we may be saved.
translator_notes: The second refrain, now with Elohim Tseva'ot ('God of hosts/armies') — a step up from the simple Elohim of verse 4. The psalmist is invoking God's military power: the God who commands angelic armies.

verses.text_hebrew: גֶּ֭פֶן מִמִּצְרַ֣יִם תַּסִּ֑יעַ תְּגָרֵ֥שׁ גּ֝וֹיִ֗ם וַתִּטָּעֶֽהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast brought a vine out of Egypt: thou hast cast out the heathen, and planted it.
verses.rendering:
You uprooted a vine from Egypt;
You drove out nations and planted it.
verses.expanded_rendering: The vine allegory begins. The gefen ('vine, grapevine') is Israel, transplanted mi-Mitsrayim ('from Egypt') — the Exodus is described as the digging up and replanting of a vine. The verb tassia ('You uprooted, You caused to journey') uses the language of transplantation. God then tegaresh goyim ('drove out nations') — cleared the land of its previous inhabitants — vattitta'eha ('and You planted it'). The entire history of the Exodus and conquest is compressed into a single horticultural operation: uproot, clear, plant.
translator_notes: The vine as metaphor for Israel appears in Isaiah 5:1-7 (the most extensive version), Jeremiah 2:21, Ezekiel 15:1-8, and Hosea 10:1. It is one of the most common metaphors for the covenant people in the prophetic tradition.

key_terms.semantic_range: grapevine, vine; by metaphor, the covenant people of Israel
key_terms.note: The grapevine was Israel's most valued cultivated plant — source of wine, raisins, and shade. When used as a metaphor for Israel, it emphasizes both the care God invested in the people and the productivity He expected in return.

verses.text_hebrew: פִּנִּ֥יתָ לְפָנֶ֑יהָ וַתַּשְׁרֵ֥שׁ שׇׁ֝רָשֶׁ֗יהָ וַתְּמַלֵּא־אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou preparedst room before it, and didst cause it to take deep root, and it filled the land.
verses.rendering:
You cleared the ground before it;
it took deep root and filled the land.
translator_notes: The verb pinnita ('You cleared') means God prepared the soil — He removed obstacles so the vine could thrive. The phrase vattashresh shorasheiha ('and it rooted its roots') describes deep establishment. The result: vattemalle erets ('it filled the land'). The vine spread across the entire territory — the land of Canaan became fully Israelite.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּסּ֣וּ הָרִ֣ים צִלָּ֑הּ וַ֝עֲנָפֶ֗יהָ אַֽרְזֵי־אֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: The hills were covered with the shadow of it, and the boughs thereof were like the goodly cedars.
verses.rendering:
The mountains were covered with its shade,
and its branches were like the cedars of God.
verses.expanded_rendering: The vine grew beyond all normal proportion: its shade covered mountains, and its branches (anafeiha) reached the stature of arzei El ('cedars of God' — the greatest, most magnificent cedars, so called because only God could grow trees that large). This is the Davidic-Solomonic golden age in agricultural imagery: Israel's influence was so great that it overshadowed everything around it.
translator_notes: The phrase arzei El ('cedars of God') uses the divine name as a superlative — 'mighty cedars, cedars of extraordinary size.' The same construction appears in Psalm 36:7 (hare El, 'mountains of God') and Psalm 68:16 (har Elohim, 'mountain of God'). God's name marks the superlative.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּשַׁלַּ֣ח קְצִירֶ֣הָ עַד־יָ֑ם וְאֶל־נָ֝הָ֗ר יוֹנְקוֹתֶֽיהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: She sent out her boughs unto the sea, and her branches unto the river.
verses.rendering:
It sent out its branches to the Sea
and its shoots to the River.
verses.expanded_rendering: The vine's reach now matches the ideal boundaries of the promised land: ad yam ('to the Sea' — the Mediterranean, the western boundary) and el nahar ('to the River' — the Euphrates, the eastern/northern boundary). These are the exact boundaries promised to Abraham (Genesis 15:18) and described in Psalm 72:8. The vine grew to fill the entire covenanted territory. This is the high point of the allegory — and it makes the reversal in the next verse all the more devastating.
translator_notes: The territorial extent described here matches the idealized borders of Solomon's kingdom (1 Kings 4:21, 24). The vine allegory reads Israel's history as a growth narrative that peaked under David and Solomon.

verses.text_hebrew: לָ֭מָּה פָּרַ֣צְתָּ גְדֵרֶ֑יהָ וְ֝אָר֗וּהָ כׇּל־עֹבְרֵ֥י דָֽרֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Why hast thou then broken down her hedges, so that all they which pass by the way do pluck her?
verses.rendering:
Why have You broken down its walls,
so that all who pass along the road pluck its fruit?
verses.expanded_rendering: The pivot is abrupt and anguished: lammah paratsta gedereiha ('why have You broken down its walls?'). The gederot ('stone walls, fences') are the protective boundaries that kept the vineyard safe — and God broke them. Not the enemy, not the vine's failure — God Himself tore down the protection. Now kol ovrei derekh ('all who pass by on the road') — casual travelers, not even deliberate enemies — help themselves to the vine's fruit. The vineyard is open to plunder because the Owner removed the fence.
translator_notes: Isaiah 5:5 uses nearly identical language: 'I will remove its hedge... I will break down its wall.' The parallel confirms that the removal of divine protection is understood as a deliberate act of God, not an accident or an enemy's achievement.

verses.text_hebrew: יְכַרְסְמֶ֣נָּה חֲזִ֣יר מִיָּ֑עַר וְזִ֥יז שָׂ֝דַ֗י יִרְעֶֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: The boar out of the wood doth waste it, and the wild beast of the field doth devour it.
verses.rendering:
The boar from the forest ravages it,
and the creatures of the field feed on it.
verses.expanded_rendering: With the walls broken, predators enter. The chazir miyya'ar ('boar from the forest') is an unclean animal (Leviticus 11:7) that destroys vineyards by rooting through the ground and tearing up the roots. The ziz sadai ('swarming creatures of the field') consume what remains. The vine that once overshadowed cedars is now food for pigs and insects. The description of Israel's enemies as unclean animals adds a layer of disgust — the holy vine is being consumed by the ritually impure.
translator_notes: In the Masoretic text, the word ya'ar ('forest') has a suspended letter ayin, one of only a handful of such scribal marks in the Hebrew Bible. This may mark the midpoint of the Psalter by letter count, or it may preserve a textual variant.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֣ים צְבָא֣וֹת שֽׁוּב־נָ֑א הַבֵּ֥ט מִ֝שָּׁמַ֗יִם וּרְאֵ֗ה וּפְקֹ֥ד גֶּ֣פֶן זֹֽאת׃
verses.text_kjv: Return, we beseech thee, O God of hosts: look down from heaven, and behold, and visit this vine;
verses.expanded_rendering: The plea is urgent: shuv na ('turn back, please!') asks God to reverse His direction — He turned away, now turn back. Habbet mishamayim ('look down from heaven') is the same language as Deuteronomy 26:15, where Israel asks God to look from His holy dwelling and bless the land. The verb ufqod ('and visit, and attend to, and take account of') is loaded: paqad means to visit with intent — to inspect, to care for, to intervene. The psalmist asks God to come back and tend gefen zot ('this vine') — His own vine, the one He planted.
translator_notes: The verb paqad ('to visit, to attend to') can mean either to bless or to punish, depending on context. Here it means 'to take care of, to restore' — the vine needs the gardener's attention, not His absence.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭כַנָּה אֲשֶׁר־נָטְעָ֣ה יְמִינֶ֑ךָ וְעַל־בֵּ֝֗ן אִמַּ֥צְתָּה לָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: And the vineyard which thy right hand hath planted, and the branch that thou madest strong for thyself.
verses.rendering:
Protect what Your right hand has planted,
the shoot You have strengthened for Yourself.
translator_notes: The word khannah ('stock, root, vineyard plot') refers to the planted area. The phrase asher nat'ah yeminekha ('that Your right hand planted') attributes the vine's existence to God's own direct action. The ben ('son, shoot, branch') that God immatsettah lakh ('strengthened for Yourself') may be a double reference: the vine's strongest branch (the Davidic dynasty) and/or the nation itself as God's 'son' (cf. Exodus 4:22, 'Israel is My firstborn son').

verses.text_hebrew: שְׂרֻפָ֣ה בָ֭אֵשׁ כְּסוּחָ֑ה מִגַּעֲרַ֖ת פָּנֶ֣יךָ יֹאבֵֽדוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: It is burned with fire, it is cut down: they perish at the rebuke of thy countenance.
verses.rendering:
It is burned with fire, cut down;
at the rebuke of Your face they perish.
translator_notes: The vine that once covered mountains is now serufah va-esh ('burned with fire') and khesukhah ('cut down, pruned to the stump'). The phrase migga'arat panekha ('from the rebuke of Your face') attributes even the burning and cutting to God's own action — His face that was asked to shine (verse 4) has instead rebuked. The same face that could save is the face that destroys.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּהִי־יָ֭דְךָ עַל־אִ֣ישׁ יְמִינֶ֑ךָ עַל־בֶּן־אָ֝דָ֗ם אִמַּ֥צְתָּ לָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let thy hand be upon the man of thy right hand, upon the son of man whom thou madest strong for thyself.
verses.rendering:
Let Your hand be upon the man at Your right hand,
upon the son of man whom You strengthened for Yourself.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase ish yeminekha ('the man of Your right hand') is the one God has placed in the position of power and favor. This likely refers to the Davidic king, who sits at God's right hand (Psalm 110:1). The parallel ben adam ('son of man') is the standard Hebrew phrase for 'human being' but here carries royal overtones — it refers to the same figure, the God-appointed leader of the people. The phrase immatsettah lakh ('You strengthened for Yourself') echoes verse 16 — the same verb applied to both the vine-branch and the human leader. God is asked to protect the king He Himself installed.
translator_notes: The phrase ben adam ('son of man') here is not the apocalyptic 'Son of Man' of Daniel 7:13, but it contributes to the larger biblical tradition of the term. In this psalm, it refers to the human leader whom God chose and empowered — the Davidic king as representative of the people.

key_terms.semantic_range: a human being, mortal, a person; in context, the representative human whom God has appointed
key_terms.note: Ben adam literally means 'son of Adam' or 'son of humanity.' In Ezekiel, God repeatedly addresses the prophet as ben adam to emphasize his humanity. Here it refers to the king as God's chosen human agent — a mortal who bears divine appointment.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלֹא־נָס֥וֹג מִמֶּ֑ךָּ תְּ֝חַיֵּ֗נוּ וּבְשִׁמְךָ֥ נִקְרָֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: So will not we go back from thee: quicken us, and we will call upon thy name.
verses.rendering:
Then we will not turn back from You;
revive us, and we will call on Your name.
verses.expanded_rendering: The vow is conditional but sincere: velo nasog mimmekha ('we will not turn back from You'). The verb nasog ('to retreat, to backslide, to turn away') is the opposite of the restoration being requested. The people promise that if God revives them (techayyenu, 'give us life'), they will call on His name (uveshimkha niqra, 'we will call upon Your name') — public, vocal worship. The bargain is straightforward: revive us, and we will be faithful.
translator_notes: The promise 'we will not turn back' responds to the accusation of Psalm 78:57 ('they turned back and were faithless'). The psalmist pledges that the restored people will not repeat the pattern of the ancestors.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֣ים צְבָא֣וֹת הֲשִׁיבֵ֑נוּ הָאֵ֥ר פָּ֝נֶ֗יךָ וְנִוָּשֵֽׁעָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Turn us again, O LORD God of hosts, cause thy face to shine; and we shall be saved.
verses.rendering:
Restore us, O LORD God of hosts;
let Your face shine, that we may be saved.
verses.expanded_rendering: The third and final refrain arrives with the fullest possible divine title: YHWH Elohim Tseva'ot ('LORD God of hosts'). The escalation from Elohim (verse 4) to Elohim Tseva'ot (verse 8) to YHWH Elohim Tseva'ot (verse 20) is the psalm's structural climax — every available name has now been invoked. The covenant name YHWH, the title of deity Elohim, and the military designation Tseva'ot combine to address God with maximum urgency and maximum intimacy. The psalm ends as it began: with a plea for the Shepherd's face to shine on His flock.
translator_notes: The three-part refrain structure (verses 4, 8, 20) creates the psalm's emotional architecture: the same words repeated with increasing intensity. The refrain is not merely decorative but performative — it enacts the community's desperate, repeated crying out. The third repetition, with the fullest divine name, is the psalm's final, most concentrated appeal.
